title: Path Traversal & Secure Design Principles
description: Master directory traversal attacks and defences, plus the foundational security design principles every developer must know
learning_outcomes: Explain how file systems resolve paths including relative references like ../ and symbolic links
Demonstrate path traversal attacks using dot-dot-slash sequences to escape restricted directories
Trace how user input flows from HTTP requests to file system operations in Java code
Identify path traversal vulnerabilities in Java servlets and file-handling code
Implement whitelist validation using regex to restrict filenames to safe characters
Use getCanonicalPath() and toRealPath() to validate file paths against a base directory
Recognise the trailing-slash corner case that bypasses startsWith() checks
Explain and apply the Zip Slip vulnerability and its mitigation
Define and apply the 8 Saltzer and Schroeder secure design principles
Apply defence-in-depth by combining multiple path traversal mitigations
Classify path traversal under CWE-22 and relate it to OWASP Top 10
Analyse real-world path traversal breaches and their impact

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses building/house analogies. Never judges. Celebrates every vulnerability spotted and every defence built.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey there! Path traversal is all about understanding how file systems work — like navigating floors in a building. The attacker uses ../ to go UP to floors they should not access. Want me to draw the building analogy?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still working? No worries — path traversal has many encoding tricks that make it confusing. Would you like me to: (A) Show the basic ../ attack step by step, (B) Compare vulnerable vs secure code side by side, or (C) Explain canonical paths with a real example?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Take your time! Here is the core idea: your app says 'users can only access files in /uploads/'. The attacker sends '../../../etc/passwd' as the filename. The server builds the path /uploads/../../../etc/passwd which resolves to /etc/passwd — OUTSIDE the safe directory! The fix: use getCanonicalPath() to resolve the real path and check it starts with /uploads/.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good attempt! Path traversal defences have subtle corner cases. Did you check: (1) the trailing slash on the base directory, (2) null bytes, (3) URL encoding of ../ ? Try again!
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: No problem! Let me break it down step by step. First, what path does the attacker provide? Second, what does the server build? Third, what does the canonical path resolve to? Fourth, does it still start with the base directory?
intervention_triggers.after_success: Excellent! You just blocked an attacker from reading sensitive files on your server. Path traversal is in the OWASP Top 10 and you now know how to prevent it. Real security skill!

encouragement_phrases: Path traversal is one of the oldest attacks — and it STILL works on modern applications!
You are learning to think like both the attacker AND the defender!
Great catch! That ../ would have escaped the safe directory!
The canonical path check you wrote would stop real attacks!
Secure design principles are the foundation of EVERYTHING in security!
Defence in depth means even if one check fails, the next one catches it!
Every file your app serves should be validated. You are building that skill now!
The Zip Slip attack is sneaky — and you just learned to block it!
Least privilege means the app should only access what it absolutely needs!
You are thinking about security at the DESIGN level — that is senior-developer thinking!

explanation_styles.analogy: Think of your web server as a building. The /uploads/ folder is the lobby — visitors are allowed there. The /etc/ folder is the manager's office on a restricted floor. Path traversal is like someone in the lobby pressing the elevator button for the restricted floor because nobody checked their access badge. The ../ in a filepath means 'go up one floor'. Three ../../../ takes you from the lobby all the way up to the restricted floor.
explanation_styles.visual: VULNERABLE: base=/uploads/ + input=../../../etc/passwd -> /uploads/../../../etc/passwd -> resolves to /etc/passwd (OUTSIDE uploads!). SECURE: canonical path of /uploads/../../../etc/passwd = /etc/passwd. Does /etc/passwd start with /uploads/? NO! -> BLOCKED!
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: User sends filename parameter. Step 2: Server builds path: base_dir + filename. Step 3: If filename contains ../, the path goes ABOVE the base directory. Step 4: Without validation, the server reads/serves the file. Step 5: Attacker gets /etc/passwd, config files, source code, etc.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Input: file=../../../etc/passwd. Code: new File('/uploads/', filename). Built path: /uploads/../../../etc/passwd. Canonical: /etc/passwd. startsWith('/uploads/')? FALSE. BLOCK!
explanation_styles.comparison: Whitelist: Only allow [a-zA-Z0-9._] — rejects ANY path separator. Canonical path: Resolve then compare — catches encoded and symlink bypasses. BOTH together: Defence in depth!

theory.introduction:
Imagine you work in a large office building. Your company occupies the 5th floor, and you have a keycard that should only let you access that floor. But what if the elevator had a bug where typing '-3' on your floor number took you to floor 2 — the HR department with everyone's salary data? That is path traversal.

In web applications, files are organised in directories (folders). A web app might store user uploads in /var/www/uploads/ and serve them when requested. The app builds the file path by combining the base directory with the user's requested filename: /var/www/uploads/ + photo.jpg = /var/www/uploads/photo.jpg.

But what if the user requests ../../../etc/passwd instead of photo.jpg? The server builds: /var/www/uploads/../../../etc/passwd. The ../ means 'go up one directory'. Three of them take you from /var/www/uploads/ up to / (the root), then into /etc/passwd — the Unix password file!

Path Traversal (CWE-22) is classified as a HIGH severity vulnerability. It is part of the OWASP Top 10 under Broken Access Control (A01:2025). Real-world attacks have exposed source code, configuration files with database passwords, private keys, and customer data.

In this topic you will also learn the 8 Secure Design Principles from Saltzer and Schroeder (1975) — foundational rules that apply to ALL security, not just path traversal. These principles (Economy of Mechanism, Fail-Safe Defaults, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Separation of Privilege, Least Privilege, Least Common Mechanism, and Psychological Acceptability) are referenced in every major security standard including OWASP, NIST, and ISO 27001.
theory.why_important: Path traversal can expose ANY file on your server: source code, database credentials, private encryption keys, user data, and system configuration. A single path traversal vulnerability in a web application can lead to complete server compromise. The Equifax breach (2017) involved a path traversal component that helped attackers move through the system. Understanding secure design principles prevents not just path traversal but entire categories of vulnerabilities — they are the foundation that everything else is built on.

key_concepts.concept: How File Systems Work: Paths, Directories, and Resolution
key_concepts.explanation:
A file path is an address that tells the operating system where to find a file. There are two types:

ABSOLUTE paths start from the root: /var/www/uploads/photo.jpg (Linux) or C:\Users\Public\photo.jpg (Windows)

RELATIVE paths start from the current directory: ./photo.jpg (current dir) or ../config/db.properties (up one, into config)

Special path components:
- . means 'current directory'
- .. means 'parent directory' (go UP one level)
- / is the directory separator (Linux/Mac)
- \ is the directory separator (Windows, also accepts /)

Path RESOLUTION is when the OS converts a path with ../ into its real location. /var/www/uploads/../../etc/passwd resolves step by step: /var/www/uploads/ -> /var/www/ (one ..) -> /var/ (two ..) -> /var/etc/passwd. Wait — that is wrong! Let me redo: /var/www/uploads/../.. = /var/www/.. wait no. The key: each ../ goes up one level from the current position.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Each ../ goes up one directory. Three ../../../ from /var/www/uploads/ takes you to / (root). From there, etc/passwd is the system password file.

key_concepts.explanation:
Path traversal (also called directory traversal) happens when a web application uses user input to construct a file path WITHOUT validating that the resulting path stays within the intended directory.

The attack is simple: the attacker includes ../ sequences in the filename to navigate UP from the safe directory into restricted areas of the file system.

Common targets:
- /etc/passwd — Linux user accounts
- /etc/shadow — Linux password hashes (if readable)
- /etc/hosts — Network configuration
- /var/log/ — Server logs
- Web application source code
- Configuration files with database passwords
- Private SSL/TLS keys

The attack works because the file system resolves ../ BEFORE the application can check the path. By the time the OS opens the file, the ../ has already navigated outside the safe directory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The attack works because the server trusts the filename. It builds the path and opens the file without checking if the result is still inside the safe directory.

key_concepts.explanation:
Simple filters that block ../ can be bypassed using alternative encodings:

1. URL encoding: ../ becomes %2e%2e%2f or %2e%2e/
2. Double URL encoding: ../ becomes %252e%252e%252f (the % is encoded as %25)
3. Unicode encoding: ../ can be represented as ..%c0%af or ..%ef%bc%8f
4. Backslash on Windows: ..\ works the same as ../ and can also be encoded
5. Null byte: filename.jpg%00../../../etc/passwd — the null byte (%00) may terminate the string early in some languages
6. Mixed encoding: %2e./ or .%2e/ — mixing encoded and plain characters

This is why simple string replacement (removing ../) is NOT sufficient. An attacker can encode the ../ in dozens of ways. The correct approach is to resolve the CANONICAL path and compare it to the base directory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Never try to BLOCK bad input. Instead, VALIDATE the RESULT. Resolve the canonical path and check it is still in the safe directory.

key_concepts.explanation:
The simplest and most effective first defence is to WHITELIST which characters are allowed in filenames. If you only allow letters, numbers, dots, underscores, and hyphens, then ../ CANNOT be constructed because the / character is not in the whitelist.

The regex pattern [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+ matches only safe filename characters. Any input containing / or \ or .. fails the validation immediately.

This works well when filenames follow a predictable pattern (like uploaded files). It does NOT work when the application needs to accept paths with subdirectories (like images/photo.jpg).
key_concepts.ai_hint: Whitelist = only allow KNOWN GOOD characters. If / is not allowed, ../ cannot be constructed. Simple and effective!

key_concepts.explanation:
The canonical path is the ABSOLUTE, RESOLVED, NORMALISED path to a file. It removes all ../ references, resolves symbolic links, and produces the one TRUE path to the file.

The defence: build the file path, get its canonical form, and verify it starts with the base directory's canonical path.

Java provides two methods:
- File.getCanonicalPath() — resolves ../ and symlinks, returns absolute path
- Path.toRealPath() — similar but uses the newer NIO API

CRITICAL CORNER CASE: The base directory string must end with / (trailing slash). Without it, the path /safedir could match /safedirEVIL because "/safedirEVIL".startsWith("/safedir") is TRUE! Adding the trailing slash means we check startsWith("/safedir/") which does NOT match /safedirEVIL.

A SECOND corner case: the input ../safedir/ / (with a space) can fool getCanonicalPath() into keeping the trailing slash, making /safedir/ match /safedir/ even though it refers to the directory itself (which can then be deleted). The fix: also check that the canonical path length is greater than the base directory path length.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Canonical path resolves ALL tricks (../, symlinks, encoding). Always compare canonical paths, and always use trailing / on the base directory.

key_concepts.explanation:
Java's NIO (New I/O) API provides Path.toRealPath() which is the modern equivalent of getCanonicalPath(). It resolves symlinks and normalises the path.

The key method: Path.toRealPath(LinkOption... options) resolves symbolic links by default. You can also use Path.normalize() to remove ../ without resolving symlinks (useful for checking before the file exists).

For checking parent directories, use Path.startsWith(Path other) which is safer than String.startsWith() because it compares path COMPONENTS, not string prefixes. /safedir/file starts with /safedir/ but /safedirEVIL does NOT start with /safedir/ when using Path.startsWith().
key_concepts.ai_hint: Use Path.startsWith(Path) not String.startsWith(String). The Path version compares directory COMPONENTS and is immune to the /safedirEVIL trick.

key_concepts.concept: Zip Slip: Path Traversal via Archive Extraction
key_concepts.explanation:
Zip Slip is a clever path traversal attack that works through archive files (ZIP, TAR, JAR). The attacker creates a malicious ZIP file where the internal filenames contain ../ sequences. When the application extracts the ZIP, it writes files OUTSIDE the extraction directory.

For example, a ZIP file might contain an entry named: ../../../../tmp/evil.sh. When extracted to /var/www/uploads/, the file gets written to /tmp/evil.sh instead.

This is especially dangerous because:
1. The malicious path is INSIDE the archive, not in the URL
2. Many ZIP extraction libraries do NOT check for path traversal
3. The attacker can overwrite any file the application has write access to
4. Overwriting a shell script or configuration file can lead to Remote Code Execution

The fix: for every entry in the ZIP, resolve the canonical path of the destination and verify it starts with the extraction directory. Reject any entry that would write outside the safe directory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Zip Slip hides path traversal INSIDE archive files. Always validate every zip entry's destination path before extracting.

key_concepts.concept: Secure Design Principle 1: Economy of Mechanism
key_concepts.explanation:
Keep it simple. The simpler the security mechanism, the fewer bugs it will have and the easier it is to review. Complex code has more places for vulnerabilities to hide.

In practice: use well-tested libraries instead of writing your own crypto. Keep your codebase small. Remove unnecessary features. Every line of code is a potential vulnerability.

Applied to path traversal: a simple whitelist regex ([a-zA-Z0-9._-]+) is easier to verify than a complex filter that tries to detect and remove every possible encoding of ../.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Simple code = fewer bugs = fewer vulnerabilities. If you can explain your security in one sentence, it's probably good.

key_concepts.explanation:
The default should be to DENY access. Only explicitly grant access when all checks pass. If any check fails, if there is an error, if something unexpected happens — the default answer is NO.

This is the opposite of fail-open, where errors cause the system to allow access. Fail-open is one of the most dangerous design flaws in security.

Applied to path traversal: if the canonical path check throws an exception (maybe the file does not exist), the application should DENY access, not serve a default file or skip the check.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Default = DENY. Only allow when ALL checks pass. If anything goes wrong, the answer is NO.

key_concepts.explanation:
Every access to every resource must be checked EVERY time. Do not cache authorisation decisions. Do not assume that because a user was allowed to access a file once, they should be allowed again.

Applied to path traversal: validate the file path on EVERY request, not just the first time. The file system can change between requests (files moved, symlinks created). A path that was safe yesterday might be dangerous today.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Check EVERY access, EVERY time. Never cache security decisions. The situation can change between requests.

key_concepts.explanation:
Your security should not depend on the design being SECRET. The system should be secure even if attackers know exactly how it works. Security through obscurity is not security.

In cryptography: the algorithm (AES, RSA) is public. Only the KEY is secret. Even if the attacker knows the algorithm, they cannot break it without the key.

Applied to path traversal: your validation code should work even if the attacker can read your source code. A whitelist regex or canonical path check works regardless of whether the attacker knows about it. A blacklist of specific attack patterns fails when the attacker finds a pattern you didn't think of.
key_concepts.ai_hint: If your security only works because attackers don't know how it works, it WILL be broken. Design security that works even when the attacker reads your code.

key_concepts.explanation:
Every program, process, and user should operate with the MINIMUM permissions needed to complete their task. No more, no less.

For web applications: the web server process should NOT run as root. It should only have read access to the directories it needs to serve. It should have write access ONLY to upload directories. It should have NO access to /etc/shadow, private keys, or system configuration.

Applied to path traversal: even if a path traversal vulnerability exists, if the web server process only has read access to /var/www/, it CANNOT read /etc/passwd because the OS blocks it. Least privilege is a safety net that limits damage.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Least privilege = the app only gets the minimum access it needs. Even if attacked, the damage is contained.

key_concepts.concept: Secure Design Principle 6: Separation of Privilege
key_concepts.explanation:
Require more than one condition to be met before granting access. Like a bank vault that needs two keys turned simultaneously — no single person can open it alone.

Applied to path traversal defence-in-depth:
1. First check: whitelist validation (filename characters)
2. Second check: canonical path validation (result location)
3. Third check: OS-level file permissions (least privilege)

All three must pass. Even if one check has a bug, the others catch the attack.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Multiple checks = multiple layers. If one fails, the others protect you. This is defence in depth.

key_concepts.explanation:
Path traversal is not theoretical — it has caused major real-world breaches:

1. Apache HTTP Server (CVE-2021-41773): A path traversal bug allowed attackers to read any file on the server by using %2e (URL-encoded dot) to bypass the path check. Affected thousands of servers worldwide.

2. Spring Cloud Function (CVE-2022-22963): Path traversal in the routing functionality allowed remote code execution.

3. Zip Slip (2018): Researchers at Snyk found path traversal in archive extraction in thousands of projects including HP, Amazon, Apache, and LinkedIn. The vulnerability affected libraries in Java, .NET, Ruby, JavaScript, and Go.

4. Fortinet VPN (CVE-2018-13379): Path traversal in the FortiOS SSL VPN web portal allowed unauthenticated attackers to download system files, exposing VPN credentials. Used in real attacks against government agencies.

These breaches show that path traversal is current, dangerous, and actively exploited.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Path traversal is actively exploited. CVE-2021-41773 affected thousands of Apache servers. These are not theoretical attacks.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Basic Path Traversal Attack
complete_examples.description: A simple file download servlet vulnerable to path traversal. We trace the attack from URL to file system.
complete_examples.output:
Normal: Serves /var/www/uploads/photo.jpg (intended)
Attack: Serves /etc/passwd (system file exposed!)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The servlet takes the 'file' parameter and concatenates it directly with the upload directory. No validation means the attacker can use ../ to navigate anywhere on the file system. The OS resolves the ../ sequences before opening the file.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How many ../ would you need to reach the root from /var/www/uploads/? (Answer: 3 — up from uploads, up from www, up from var)

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Whitelist Defence
complete_examples.description: Adding regex whitelist validation to block path traversal characters.
complete_examples.output:
photo.jpg -> Allowed (safe characters only)
../../../etc/passwd -> BLOCKED (/ not in whitelist)
All encoding bypasses also blocked (special chars not in whitelist)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The regex [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+ only allows letters, numbers, dots, underscores, and hyphens. Since / and \ and % are not in the whitelist, no path traversal sequence can be constructed. This is the simplest and most effective first defence.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the application needs to serve files in subdirectories like images/photo.jpg? The whitelist blocks / so this would fail. How would you handle it?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Canonical Path Validation
complete_examples.description: Using getCanonicalPath() to catch path traversal after resolving all ../ and symlinks.
complete_examples.output:
photo.jpg -> Canonical: /var/www/uploads/photo.jpg, starts with base -> ALLOWED
../../../etc/passwd -> Canonical: /etc/passwd, does NOT start with base -> BLOCKED
%2e%2e%2fetc/passwd -> Canonical: /etc/passwd -> BLOCKED (decoded before canonical)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: getCanonicalPath() resolves ALL tricks: ../, symlinks, URL encoding. The canonical path is the TRUE location of the file. By comparing it to the base directory's canonical path, we ensure the file is inside the allowed directory regardless of how the attacker constructed the input.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What is the trailing slash corner case? What happens if BASE = '/var/www/uploads' (no trailing slash)?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: The Trailing Slash Corner Case
complete_examples.description: Demonstrating why the base directory MUST end with / to prevent the /safedirEVIL bypass.
complete_examples.output:
Without trailing /: /safedirEVIL starts with /safedir = TRUE (BYPASSED!)
With trailing /: /safedirEVIL does NOT start with /safedir/ = FALSE (BLOCKED!)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: String.startsWith() checks character by character. '/safedirEVIL'.startsWith('/safedir') is TRUE because the first 8 characters match. Adding the trailing slash means we check '/safedirEVIL'.startsWith('/safedir/') which is FALSE because the 9th character is E, not /.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Java NIO's Path.startsWith(Path) does NOT have this bug. Why? Because it compares path COMPONENTS, not characters.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Java NIO Path Validation
complete_examples.description: Modern path validation using Java NIO which is safer than File.getCanonicalPath().
complete_examples.output:
photo.jpg -> Resolves within BASE -> ALLOWED
../../../etc/passwd -> normalize removes ../, not in BASE -> BLOCKED
/safedirEVIL -> Path.startsWith checks components -> BLOCKED (no trailing / needed!)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Java NIO Path.startsWith(Path) compares path COMPONENTS (directory names), not string characters. This means /safedirEVIL does NOT start with /safedir because 'safedirEVIL' is a different component than 'safedir'. This eliminates the trailing slash corner case entirely.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What does toRealPath() do that normalize() does not? (Answer: toRealPath resolves symbolic links, normalize only removes ../)

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Zip Slip Attack and Defence
complete_examples.description: Demonstrating the Zip Slip vulnerability where malicious archive entries write files outside the extraction directory.
complete_examples.output:
Vulnerable: ../../../tmp/evil.sh extracts to /tmp/evil.sh (OUTSIDE dest!)
Secure: Canonical path check catches it -> SecurityException thrown
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Zip Slip is particularly dangerous because the malicious path is hidden INSIDE the archive file. The extraction code must validate EVERY entry's destination path against the extraction directory before writing any file.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What could an attacker overwrite with Zip Slip to achieve Remote Code Execution? (Hint: cron jobs, .bashrc, web shell in the web root)

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Fail-Safe vs Fail-Open
complete_examples.description: Comparing dangerous fail-open error handling with correct fail-safe design.
complete_examples.output:
Fail-open: Exception -> serves file without validation -> VULNERABLE
Fail-safe: Exception -> denies access -> SECURE
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Fail-safe means the default is to DENY access. If any check fails or throws an exception, the result is denial. Fail-open means errors cause the system to skip security checks and allow access — this is extremely dangerous.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Think of a real-world fail-safe: a building's fire doors are CLOSED by default and only open when the fire alarm triggers an unlock. What would fail-open look like?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Complete Mediation - Check Every Request
complete_examples.description: Why you must validate every single file access, not cache validation results.
complete_examples.output:
Cached: symlink attack bypasses validation on second request
Every-time check: symlink caught because toRealPath() resolves it
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Complete Mediation means checking EVERY access, EVERY time. File systems can change between requests: files moved, symlinks created, permissions changed. A path that was safe at 10:00 might be dangerous at 10:01.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you think of another scenario where caching a security decision is dangerous? (Hint: user permissions that were revoked between requests)

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Least Privilege for Web Applications
complete_examples.description: Configuring OS-level permissions so path traversal has limited impact.
complete_examples.output:
As root: path traversal = total compromise
As webapp user: path traversal = only publicly readable files (damage limited)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Least privilege is a safety net. Even if your code has a path traversal vulnerability, the OS prevents the application from accessing files it does not have permission to read. Running as a limited user dramatically reduces the impact of path traversal.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other principle does this demonstrate? (Answer: Defence in depth — multiple layers of protection)

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Path Traversal in Windows vs Linux
complete_examples.description: Platform differences in path separators and how they affect path traversal attacks.
complete_examples.output:
Linux: ../ with / separator targets /etc/passwd
Windows: ..\ with \ separator targets C:\Windows\win.ini
Windows also accepts / so both work!
Defence: whitelist blocks both separators
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Windows accepts BOTH / and \ as path separators, which means a filter that only checks for / will miss ..\ attacks on Windows. The safest approach is a whitelist that blocks ALL characters except known-safe ones.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Java's File.separator returns / on Linux and \ on Windows. How does this affect your validation code if it runs on both platforms?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Encoding Table - All Bypass Representations
complete_examples.description: Complete reference of how ../ can be encoded in different ways to bypass filters.
complete_examples.output:
15+ ways to encode ../ - blacklisting cannot catch them all
Canonical path resolves ALL of them to the same real path
Always validate the RESULT, not the INPUT
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This table shows why blacklisting (trying to block specific attack patterns) is fundamentally flawed for path traversal. There are too many ways to encode the same thing. Canonical path validation works because it checks the RESULT after the OS resolves all encoding.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: A WAF (Web Application Firewall) blocks requests containing ../. Can you construct a path traversal payload that bypasses it? (Hint: double encoding)

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Design Principles Applied to a File Server
complete_examples.description: Applying all 8 Saltzer & Schroeder principles to design a secure file server.
complete_examples.output:
All 8 principles applied:
1. Economy: simple regex
2. Fail-safe: errors = deny
3. Complete mediation: every request validated
4. Open design: code is secure even if public
5. Separation: multiple checks
6. Least privilege: read-only, size limit
7. Least common: isolated server
8. Psychological: simple user interface
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This example shows how all 8 design principles work together in a single file server implementation. Each principle addresses a different aspect of security, and together they create a robust defence that is simple, verifiable, and effective.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Which principle do you think is most often violated in real applications? (Most common: fail-safe defaults — developers handle errors by allowing access instead of denying it)

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Path Traversal in Command Execution
complete_examples.description: Path traversal in a program that executes user-specified binaries from a safe directory.
complete_examples.output:
Vulnerable: ../unsafe_program executes outside safe_programs/
Weak: checks \ but not / on Windows -> bypassed!
Secure: whitelist + parent check -> blocks all traversal
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This example from a command execution context shows that path traversal is not limited to file downloads. Any code that builds a path from user input is vulnerable. The secure version uses both whitelist validation and parent directory verification.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the safe_programs directory contains a symbolic link to an unsafe program? Would the parent check catch it? (Hint: toRealPath() resolves symlinks)

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Apache CVE-2021-41773 Analysis
complete_examples.description: Analysis of the real Apache HTTP Server path traversal vulnerability that affected thousands of servers.
complete_examples.output:
CVE-2021-41773: Apache path traversal via URL encoding bypass
Check ran before decoding: %2e%2e/ passed check, then decoded to ../
Fix: Always decode before checking, or use canonical path validation
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This real-world vulnerability perfectly illustrates why the ORDER of operations matters. Apache checked for ../ before URL-decoding, so %2e%2e/ bypassed the check. The canonical path approach avoids this because it resolves everything FIRST, then validates the result.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: The fix for CVE-2021-41773 was incomplete — CVE-2021-42013 was found shortly after using double encoding (%252e). What lesson does this teach about patching?

visual_diagram.title: Path Traversal & Secure Design: Attack Flow, Defences & Design Principles
visual_diagram.content:
PATH TRAVERSAL & SECURE DESIGN PRINCIPLES
THE PATH TRAVERSAL ATTACK (CWE-22) 
  User Request:  GET /files?file=../../../etc/passwd
       |
       v
  Server Code:   File f = new File("/uploads/" + filename)
       |
       v
  Built Path:    /uploads/../../../etc/passwd
       |
       v
  OS Resolves:   /uploads/ -> / (up 3) -> /etc/passwd
       |
       v
  FILE SERVED:    /etc/passwd  (OUTSIDE uploads!)

  The ../ means "go up one directory"
  Three ../../../ escapes from /uploads/ to root /
ENCODING BYPASS TRICKS 
  ../             Direct dot-dot-slash
  %2e%2e%2f      URL-encoded ../
  %252e%252e%252f Double URL-encoded
  ..%c0%af       Unicode encoded /
  ..\            Backslash (Windows)
  ....//         Nested (bypasses single removal)
  ..%00/         Null byte injection

  ALL of these resolve to the same thing: go up one directory!
  FIX: Don't try to block them. Validate the CANONICAL result.
3 LAYERS OF DEFENCE 
  Layer 1 WHITELIST VALIDATION
    filename.matches("[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+")
    Blocks: ../ (contains /) %2e (contains %) ..\\ (contains \\)
    Simple, fast, catches most attacks

  Layer 2 CANONICAL PATH CHECK
    String canon = file.getCanonicalPath();
    if (!canon.startsWith(baseDir + "/")) BLOCK!
    Catches ALL encoding bypasses and symlinks
    ⚠️ Remember trailing / on base directory!

  Layer 3 OS-LEVEL LEAST PRIVILEGE
    Run web app as limited user (not root)
    chmod 750 /var/www/uploads/
    Even if traversal works, OS blocks file access
CANONICAL PATH CORNER CASES 
  Corner Case 1: Missing trailing /
  Base: "/safedir"  (no trailing slash)
  Attack: "../safedirEVIL"
  Canonical: "/safedirEVIL"
  "/safedirEVIL".startsWith("/safedir") = TRUE! BYPASSED!
  Fix: Always use "/safedir/" with trailing slash

  Corner Case 2: Directory itself
  Attack: "../safedir/ /" (space + slash)
  Canonical: "/safedir/" which starts with "/safedir/"
  Passes the check! Can delete the directory!
  Fix: Check canonical length > base length + 1
8 SALTZER & SCHROEDER DESIGN PRINCIPLES 
  1. Economy of Mechanism     Keep security SIMPLE
  2. Fail-Safe Defaults      Default = DENY access
  3. Complete Mediation      Check EVERY access, EVERY time
  4. Open Design             Security works even if code is public
  5. Separation of Privilege Multiple checks required
  6. Least Privilege         Minimum permissions needed
  7. Least Common Mechanism  Don't share between privilege levels
  8. Psychological Acceptability Security must be usable
REMEMBER - PATH TRAVERSAL DEFENCE 
  1. Whitelist filename characters: [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+
  2. Canonical path check with trailing / on base
  3. Run app with least privilege (not root)
  4. Validate EVERY request (complete mediation)
  5. Fail safe: errors = DENY access

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Trace Path Resolution
practice_exercises.description: Given a starting directory and relative path, determine the absolute resolved path.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. /var/www/uploads/photo.jpg
2. /var/www/config.txt
3. /var/secret.key
4. /etc/passwd
5. /var/www/uploads/images/cat.png
6. /var/www/uploads/photo.jpg (../ cancels images/)

hints.message: ./ means current directory, ../ means go up one level.
Each ../ removes the last directory component.
From /var/www/uploads/: one ../ = /var/www/, two = /var/, three = / (root).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Count the Dot-Dot-Slashes
practice_exercises.description: How many ../ sequences needed to reach root from various directories?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. 3 (uploads->www->var->root)
2. 3 (documents->user->home->root)
3. 4 (temp->data->app->opt->root)
4. 2 (share->usr->root)
5. 5 (e->d->c->b->a->root)

hints.message: Count the directory levels between your position and root.
Each directory separator (/) represents one level.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Absolute vs Relative Paths
practice_exercises.description: Identify whether paths are absolute or relative and what they resolve to.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. ABSOLUTE (starts with /)
2. RELATIVE (starts with .., needs current dir context)
3. ABSOLUTE (starts with C:\)
4. RELATIVE (starts with ./)
5. RELATIVE (no leading / or .)
6. ABSOLUTE (starts with /)

hints.message: Absolute paths start with / (Linux) or drive letter (Windows).
Relative paths start with ./, ../, or just a name.
1:ABS, 2:REL, 3:ABS, 4:REL, 5:REL, 6:ABS

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Canonical Path Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Convert paths with ../ to their canonical (resolved) form.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. /var/www/config.txt
2. /home/user/documents/file.txt (./ removed)
3. /opt/etc/hosts (up from data, up from app)
4. /etc/passwd (up 4 from d,c,b,a)
5. /tmp/evil (up from uploads, www, var)

hints.message: Process each ../ by removing the preceding directory.
./ simply means 'current directory' and can be removed.
1:/var/www/config.txt, 2:/home/user/documents/file.txt, 3:/opt/etc/hosts, 4:/etc/passwd, 5:/tmp/evil

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Identify User Input in File Code
practice_exercises.description: Find where user input enters file system operations.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Lines 4 and 5 have path traversal risk (user input in path).
Line 6 is safe (no user input, ext is hardcoded).

hints.message: Look for where getParameter() values end up in new File().
Line 4: filename from user. Line 5: both dir AND filename from user (double risk!).
Lines 4,5 vulnerable. Line 6 safe: ext is hardcoded ".pdf", no user input.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: File Separator Differences
practice_exercises.description: Understand platform-specific path separators and their security implications.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. /
2. \
3. YES (Windows accepts both / and \)
4. /
5. \
6. YES (use \ instead)
7. YES (File.separator is \ but / also works!)

hints.message: Windows is unusual: it accepts both / and \ as separators.
File.separator only returns ONE separator, but both work on Windows.
Always check for BOTH / and \ or use a whitelist.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Write getCanonicalPath Code
practice_exercises.description: Write Java code to get the canonical path of a file and print it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
File f = new File("/uploads/../../../etc/passwd");
System.out.println(f.getPath());          // /uploads/../../../etc/passwd
System.out.println(f.getCanonicalPath()); // /etc/passwd
They differ because canonical resolves ../

hints.message: new File(path) stores the path as-is. getCanonicalPath() resolves it.
getCanonicalPath() removes all ../ and resolves symlinks.
File f = new File("/uploads/../../../etc/passwd"); f.getCanonicalPath() returns "/etc/passwd"

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: String startsWith vs Path startsWith
practice_exercises.description: Understand why Path.startsWith is safer than String.startsWith.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
String: true (character-by-character match)
Path: false (component-by-component match)
Path.startsWith compares DIRECTORY NAMES, not characters.
Path is safer — immune to the /safedirEVIL trick.

hints.message: String.startsWith checks if characters match from the beginning.
Path.startsWith checks if path COMPONENTS (directory names) match.
String: TRUE (safedirEVIL starts with safedir as characters). Path: FALSE (safedirEVIL is a different component than safedir).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: URL Decoding Before Validation
practice_exercises.description: Demonstrate why input must be URL-decoded before path validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: false (%2e%2e%2f doesn't contain ../)
Decoded: ../../etc/passwd
After: true (now contains ../)
Always decode FIRST, then validate!

hints.message: %2e is URL-encoded dot (.), %2f is URL-encoded slash (/).
The raw string has no literal ../ characters — they are encoded.
Before: false, Decoded: ../../etc/passwd, After: true. Always decode before validating!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Build a Complete Path Validator
practice_exercises.description: Write a reusable method that combines whitelist + canonical path + existence check.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding file paths is the foundation for path traversal!
practice_exercises.expected_output: null check -> whitelist regex -> build File -> canonical startsWith(base + /) -> exists check -> return File. Any failure = SecurityException.

hints.message: Start with null/empty check, then whitelist, then canonical.
String canon = new File(BASE, filename).getCanonicalPath(); if (!canon.startsWith(BASE)) throw...
Complete: null check, regex match, canonical path startsWith BASE, file.exists(), return file. All failures throw SecurityException.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Craft a Path Traversal Payload
practice_exercises.description: Write path traversal payloads for different targets.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. ../../../etc/passwd
2. ../../../etc/hosts
3. ../../log/auth.log
4. ../../../home/admin/.ssh/id_rsa
5. . (current directory listing if served)

hints.message: From /var/www/uploads/, three ../ takes you to root /.
For /var/log/, you only need two ../ (up from uploads, up from www, into log).
1: ../../../etc/passwd, 2: ../../../etc/hosts, 3: ../../log/auth.log, 4: ../../../home/admin/.ssh/id_rsa

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: URL-Encode a Traversal Payload
practice_exercises.description: Convert ../ sequences to URL-encoded form.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!

hints.message: Each character maps to %HH where HH is the hex ASCII code.
. is hex 2e, / is hex 2f, \ is hex 5c.
1: %2e%2e%2f, double: %252e%252e%252f (the % itself becomes %25).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Bypass a Simple Filter
practice_exercises.description: Craft payloads that bypass a filter removing ../.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. ....// -> filter removes inner ../ leaving ../
2. ..\ -> filter only removes ../ not ..\
3. ....//....//....// -> ../../../

hints.message: If the filter removes ../ once, nesting it means the removal creates a new ../.
....// : remove the middle ../ and you get ../
....// -> ../ (inner removed). ..\ bypasses (different separator). ....//....//....// -> ../../../

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Trace a Full Path Traversal Attack
practice_exercises.description: Step through every phase of a path traversal exploitation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Step 1: ../../../../etc/shadow
Step 2: /var/www/app/uploads/../../../../etc/shadow
Step 3: /etc/shadow
Step 4: Opens /etc/shadow
Step 5: Password hashes for all users
Step 6: Crack passwords offline -> full system access

hints.message: Each ../ removes one directory from the path.
4 levels: uploads->app->www->var->root, then into etc/shadow.
/etc/shadow contains hashed passwords. Attacker can use hashcat/john to crack them.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Path Traversal in File Upload
practice_exercises.description: Find the vulnerability in a file upload that uses the original filename.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
File written to: /var/www/html/shell.jsp (web root!)
Attacker can execute the JSP shell via browser
Fix: whitelist filename chars, use UUID instead of original name

hints.message: The original filename from the upload is attacker-controlled.
Writing to the web root means the JSP file is accessible via browser and executes as code.
Fix: never use original filename. Generate a UUID: String safeName = UUID.randomUUID() + ".pdf";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Symlink Attack Scenario
practice_exercises.description: Explain how symbolic links can bypass path validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
getCanonicalPath() returns /etc/passwd (resolves symlink)
startsWith(BASE) = FALSE -> BLOCKED!
Canonical path check catches symlink escapes.
Alternative: use toRealPath() which also resolves symlinks.

hints.message: getCanonicalPath() resolves symbolic links to their real target.
After resolving, /etc/passwd does not start with /var/www/uploads/.
Canonical path catches it! That's why canonical > simple string comparison.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Null Byte Injection
practice_exercises.description: Explain the null byte (%00) bypass technique.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Validation sees: allowed.jpg%00../../../etc/passwd (whole string)
File system sees: allowed.jpg (null byte terminates string in C)
Modern Java: NOT vulnerable (checks for null bytes since 7u40)
Old PHP: VULNERABLE (uses C string functions)

hints.message: The null byte (%00, \0) terminates strings in C.
Java strings are NOT null-terminated, but file system calls may use C functions.
Modern Java rejects null bytes. Old PHP and old Java are vulnerable.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Zip Slip Payload
practice_exercises.description: Design a malicious zip file that exploits Zip Slip.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Entry: ../../html/shell.jsp
Combined: /var/www/uploads/../../html/shell.jsp
Resolved: /var/www/html/shell.jsp
Web shell accessible at: http://site.com/shell.jsp

hints.message: The zip entry name can contain ../ sequences.
From /var/www/uploads/, two ../ takes you to /var/www/, then into html/.
Entry: ../../html/shell.jsp -> writes to /var/www/html/shell.jsp

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Windows-Specific Traversal
practice_exercises.description: Craft path traversal payloads specific to Windows systems.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. ..\..\..\Windows\win.ini
2. ../../../Windows/win.ini (/ works on Windows!)
3. %2e%2e%5c%2e%2e%5cWindows%5cwin.ini
4. Absolute path bypass: if code uses path as-is, try D:\secret.txt or D:/secret.txt

hints.message: Windows accepts both / and \ as path separators.
URL encoding of \ is %5c.
Both / and \ work. Drive letters (D:\) are absolute paths that ignore the base directory.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Full Attack Chain: Traversal to RCE
practice_exercises.description: Trace a complete path traversal attack that leads to remote code execution.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Step 2: DocumentRoot /var/www/html
Step 3: filename=../../html/shell.jsp (writes to web root)
Step 4: http://target/shell.jsp
Step 5: cmd=whoami or cmd=cat /etc/passwd

hints.message: Step 1: read the Apache config to find the DocumentRoot.
Step 2: upload with traversal to write into the web root.
Full chain: read config -> find root -> upload shell -> execute commands = RCE.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Write Whitelist Regex
practice_exercises.description: Write regex patterns to validate filenames.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. [a-z0-9]+
2. [a-zA-Z0-9.-]+
3. [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+
Tests: photo.jpg=PASS, ../etc/passwd=FAIL(/), file name.txt=FAIL(space), report-v2.pdf=PASS

hints.message: Regex [abc] means allow characters a, b, or c. + means one or more.
Dots, hyphens, underscores are safe for filenames. Slashes are NOT.
[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+ allows safe chars only. Any / or \ or .. fails.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Implement Canonical Path Check
practice_exercises.description: Write the canonical path validation in Java.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!

hints.message: getCanonicalPath() resolves all ../ and symlinks.
Add File.separator after the base to prevent the /safedirEVIL bypass.
canon starts with base+separator, catch returns false.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Fix a Vulnerable File Servlet
practice_exercises.description: Rewrite a vulnerable servlet with all defences.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: if (file==null) sendError(400); if (!file.matches regex) sendError(400); canonical check with trailing /; if (!exists) sendError(404); try/catch returns 500.

hints.message: Start with null check, then whitelist, then canonical.
Wrap everything in try-catch that returns 500 (fail-safe).
null -> whitelist -> canonical startsWith -> exists -> serve. Any failure = error response.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Fix Zip Extraction
practice_exercises.description: Add path traversal validation to ZIP extraction code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Check: outFile.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(destDir.getCanonicalPath() + File.separator)
Compare: canonical output path vs canonical destination + separator
Fail: throw SecurityException, do NOT extract the entry

hints.message: Validate each entry's destination path before writing it.
Use canonical paths for both the output file and the destination directory.
if (!outFile.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(dest.getCanonicalPath() + File.separator)) throw new SecurityException(...)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Defence in Depth: Combine All Checks
practice_exercises.description: Build a method that uses whitelist + canonical + NIO + file permissions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Layer 1: regex match [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+
Layer 2: canonical startsWith(base + separator)
Layer 3: file.exists() && file.canRead()
Layer 4: file.length() < MAX_SIZE

hints.message: Each layer catches what the others might miss.
Whitelist catches invalid chars. Canonical catches encoded bypasses. Existence prevents errors.
4 layers: regex, canonical path, exists+readable, size limit. All must pass.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Secure File Upload Handler
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete secure file upload that prevents traversal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Generate UUID filename, validate content type, check size, store with generated name only. Never use getSubmittedFileName() for the storage path.

hints.message: Generate a random filename: UUID.randomUUID() + extension.
Check content type against a whitelist (image/jpeg, image/png, etc.).
String safeName = UUID.randomUUID() + getExtension(contentType); new File(UPLOAD_DIR, safeName);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: OS-Level Least Privilege Setup
practice_exercises.description: Write Linux commands to configure least privilege for a web app.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. sudo useradd -r -s /bin/false webapp
2. sudo chown -R webapp:webapp /var/www/uploads/
3. sudo chmod 750 /var/www/uploads/
4. sudo -u webapp java -jar app.jar
5. sudo -u webapp cat /etc/shadow -> Permission denied

hints.message: useradd -r creates a system user, -s /bin/false prevents login.
chown sets ownership, chmod sets permissions (7=rwx, 5=rx, 0=none).
750 means: owner can read/write/execute, group can read/execute, others nothing.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Content-Type Validation
practice_exercises.description: Validate that uploaded files match their claimed type.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Method 1: extract extension, whitelist check (easily faked by renaming)
Method 2: MIME type from header (not reliable, attacker controls it)
Method 3: Read first bytes, compare to known signatures (most reliable)
Best: magic bytes + extension whitelist

hints.message: File extensions can be changed by the attacker.
Content-Type headers are sent by the client — attacker-controlled.
Magic bytes (file signatures) are in the file content — hardest to fake. Combine with extension whitelist.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Chroot and Sandbox
practice_exercises.description: Explain how chroot isolation limits path traversal damage.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Inside chroot, / = /var/www/ (on the real filesystem)
Traversal resolves to /var/www/etc/passwd (trapped in chroot!)
Cannot access real /etc/passwd (root is changed)
Limitations: root user can escape chroot. Modern: Docker containers, seccomp, AppArmor.

hints.message: chroot changes what / means for a process.
Even ../ up to root only reaches /var/www/ because that IS root inside the chroot.
Chroot + least privilege = strong containment. Docker is the modern equivalent.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Write a Security Audit for File Access Code
practice_exercises.description: Audit code for ALL path traversal vulnerabilities and recommend fixes.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you build the defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Line 1-2: No input validation (dir AND file from user)
2. Line 3: Direct concatenation (both dir and file can traverse)
3. Line 4: No canonical path check
4. Line 5: Only checks existence, not location
5. Line 8: Information disclosure (path in error message)
6. No file type/size validation
7. No error handling (fail-safe missing)

hints.message: Both dir AND file parameters are user-controlled.
Line 8 leaks the full file path in the error message — information disclosure!
5+ issues: no validation on 2 params, no canonical check, info disclosure in error, no type/size check, no try-catch.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Match Principles to Definitions
practice_exercises.description: Match each Saltzer & Schroeder principle to its definition.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!

hints.message: Economy = simple. Fail-safe = deny by default.
Complete mediation = check everything. Open design = no secret algorithms.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Identify Principle Violations
practice_exercises.description: For each code snippet, identify which design principle is violated.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Least Privilege
2. Complete Mediation
3. Economy of Mechanism
4. Open Design
5. Fail-Safe Defaults
6. Separation of Privilege
7. Psychological Acceptability
8. Least Common Mechanism

hints.message: Root access = too many permissions = Least Privilege.
Caching = not checking every time = Complete Mediation.
All 8: LP, CM, EoM, OD, FSD, SoP, PA, LCM.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Apply Economy of Mechanism
practice_exercises.description: Simplify a complex security function.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
return input.matches("[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+") ? input : null;
OR: validate canonical path (2 lines instead of 10+)

hints.message: The complex version tries to block every possible encoding — and probably misses some.
A whitelist regex is 1 line and blocks everything not explicitly allowed.
Simple whitelist or canonical path check. 1-2 lines instead of 10+.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Design a Fail-Safe Login
practice_exercises.description: Fix a login that fails open.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!

hints.message: Fail-safe means errors = deny access.
return true in the catch block means database errors grant access!
Change to return false. Also add logging: log.severe("Auth DB error: " + e);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Apply Complete Mediation to API
practice_exercises.description: Add per-request validation to a REST API.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Problem: cached permissions don't reflect changes (user still has old permissions).
Fix: check permissions on EVERY request from the database, not from cache.
Or: set a short TTL on the cache (e.g., 5 minutes).

hints.message: If an admin revokes a user's permissions, the cache still has the old permissions.
Complete Mediation: check every access, every time. No caching of security decisions.
Load permissions from DB on each request. Cache with short TTL if performance is needed.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Design with Open Design in Mind
practice_exercises.description: Explain why security through obscurity fails.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Not secure if leaked — anyone can generate valid keys (X_ + 30 chars).
Not Open Design — security depends on the algorithm being secret.
Fix: use cryptographic key generation (random bytes + HMAC). Works even if algorithm is known.

hints.message: If the attacker reads the code, can they bypass the security?
Open Design means the security works even when the attacker knows everything except the key.
Cryptographic keys: even knowing the algorithm, you need the key to forge valid tokens.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Separation of Privilege: Two-Factor
practice_exercises.description: Design a system that requires multiple factors.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Factor 1: Password authentication
Factor 2: TOTP code (authenticator app)
File deletion: 1. Authenticated session, 2. Confirmation prompt, 3. Audit log entry before deletion.

hints.message: Separation of Privilege: no single factor should grant access to critical operations.
Two-factor: something you know (password) + something you have (authenticator).
For destructive operations: auth + confirmation + logging.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Least Common Mechanism in Microservices
practice_exercises.description: Explain why shared components between privilege levels are dangerous.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Risk: vulnerability in user component can escalate to admin (shared mechanisms).
Benefit: compromise of user component cannot affect admin (isolation).

hints.message: Shared mechanisms create paths for privilege escalation.
If admin and user share a session handler, a session bug affects both.
Separation limits blast radius. User compromise stays contained.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Psychological Acceptability: Usable Security
practice_exercises.description: Design security that users actually use instead of bypassing.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Fails because users can't comply -> sticky notes, simple patterns, password resets.
Better: passphrase-based (4+ random words), change only when compromised, lockout with unlock mechanism, MFA reduces password complexity needs.

hints.message: Security that's too hard to use gets bypassed by the users.
NIST 800-63 now recommends AGAINST forced password rotation.
Usable security: passphrases, MFA, reasonable lockout. Users comply because it's not painful.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Apply ALL 8 Principles to One System
practice_exercises.description: Design a file sharing system applying every principle.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Design principles are the foundation of ALL security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Simple whitelist validation, no complex filters
2. Default = no sharing, must explicitly grant
3. Check permissions on every download
4. Use standard crypto (AES), not custom
5. Upload requires auth + email verification
6. Read-only access unless write explicitly granted
7. Separate storage for public and private files
8. One-click sharing with clear permission indicators

hints.message: Think about how each principle applies to file sharing.
Fail-safe: files are private by default, sharing is opt-in.
All 8: simple validation, default deny, per-request checks, standard crypto, multi-factor for sensitive ops, minimum permissions, separate storage, usable interface.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Find Path Traversal in Servlet
practice_exercises.description: Identify path traversal vulnerabilities in servlet code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line 3: img from user concatenated into path without validation.
Attacker: img=../../../etc/passwd
Fix: whitelist regex + canonical path check.

hints.message: Line 1 takes user input. Line 3 puts it in a file path.
No validation between getParameter and new File.
Line 3 vulnerable. Fix: validate img with regex + canonical check.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Find Traversal in Download Handler
practice_exercises.description: Audit a file download method for all issues.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. name not validated (traversal in filename)
2. type not validated (traversal in directory)
3. No canonical path check
4. Content-Disposition header injection
5. Error returns 200 instead of error code
6. Error message exposes filename and exception details
7. No file type/size validation

hints.message: Both name AND type are user-controlled and go into the file path.
The catch block returns 200 OK and leaks error details — multiple issues.
5+ issues: both params unvalidated, no canonical check, header injection, wrong status code, info disclosure.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Fix a File API Endpoint
practice_exercises.description: Rewrite a vulnerable REST API endpoint to be secure.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Add: whitelist validation, normalize + startsWith check, existence check, try-catch with 403/404 responses.

hints.message: The @PathVariable filename goes directly into path resolution.
Even in Spring, you need manual path traversal validation.
Validate regex, normalize path, check startsWith(baseDir), catch exceptions -> 403.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Review ZIP Upload Handler
practice_exercises.description: Find Zip Slip vulnerability in upload processing code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Issue: No validation of entry.getName() — Zip Slip vulnerability.
Fix: Check out.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(destDir.getCanonicalPath() + File.separator) for every entry.

hints.message: entry.getName() can contain ../../../ path traversal.
Every zip entry must be validated before extraction.
Add canonical path check before mkdirs() and FileOutputStream.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Spot All Issues in Config Reader
practice_exercises.description: Audit configuration file loading code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. configName not validated (path traversal)
2. System property could be attacker-controlled
3. Error leaks full path and exception
4. Empty Properties returned on error may cause downstream issues
5. No canonical path validation
6. fis not in try-with-resources (resource leak on error)

hints.message: configName goes straight into the file path.
The error message prints the full path AND the exception.
4+ issues: no validation, info disclosure, fail-open (empty config instead of failing), resource leak.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Review Path Validation Code
practice_exercises.description: Find the bug in a path validation implementation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Bug: base has no trailing slash!
Attack: filename = ../uploadsEVIL/file.txt
Canonical: /var/www/uploadsEVIL/file.txt
startsWith("/var/www/uploads") = TRUE!
Fix: base = "/var/www/uploads/" (add trailing slash)

hints.message: Look at the base directory string. What's missing?
Without trailing /, uploadsEVIL starts with uploads.
Missing trailing /. Fix: "/var/www/uploads/" or use Path.startsWith(Path) which is component-based.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Fix Insecure File Deletion
practice_exercises.description: Secure a file deletion endpoint against path traversal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attack deletes /etc/important.conf — server misconfiguration!
Critical: attacker can delete ANY file the app user can write to.
Fix: whitelist regex + canonical path check + confirm file is in /uploads/.

hints.message: DELETE is even more dangerous than READ — data destruction.
Path traversal + delete = attacker can remove config files, logs, other users' data.
Validate, canonical check, audit log every deletion.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Complete Security Audit Report
practice_exercises.description: Perform a full security audit of a file management class.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALL 4 methods have path traversal (read, write, delete, list).
No input validation anywhere.
No canonical path checks.
write allows overwriting any file.
delete allows deleting any file.
list allows directory listing anywhere.
No file size limits.
No error handling (exceptions propagate).

hints.message: Every method takes a user string and puts it directly in a path.
4 methods x path traversal = 4 vulnerabilities minimum.
Every method needs: validation, canonical check, error handling. write/delete need extra authorization.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Production Hardening Checklist
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete checklist for hardening a file-serving application.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Code: 1.Whitelist input validation 2.Canonical path check 3.File type/size limits
Server: 4.Run as limited user 5.Restrict directory permissions
Monitoring: 6.Log all file access attempts 7.Alert on path traversal patterns

hints.message: Think about code defences, OS defences, and detection.
Multiple layers: validation, path check, permissions, logging.
7 items: input validation, canonical check, type/size limits, limited user, chmod, logging, alerting.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Incident Response: Path Traversal Detected
practice_exercises.description: Plan response to a detected path traversal attack.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security auditing is a critical professional skill!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Block the attacking IP, take endpoint offline
2. /etc/passwd exposed (user list), db.properties exposed (DB credentials!)
3. /etc/shadow blocked (OS permissions saved us — least privilege!)
4. No input validation on 'file' parameter
5. Rotate DB credentials immediately, patch the endpoint
6. Add whitelist + canonical check + WAF rules + monitoring

hints.message: 200 OK means the file was served. 403 means it was blocked.
db.properties likely contains database username and password!
Immediate: block + offline. DB creds compromised. Rotate immediately. Patch code.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Apache CVE-2021-41773
practice_exercises.description: Analyse the Apache path traversal CVE.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Apache 2.4.49
2. Path check ran before URL decoding
3. /icons/%2e%2e/%2e%2e/etc/passwd
4. Double encoding bypassed: %252e%252e
5. Canonical path check (decode first, then validate)

hints.message: The URL was decoded AFTER the security check.
The fix for 41773 only added single-decoding, so double-encoding bypassed it.
Decode -> check -> canonical path. Order matters!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Zip Slip Across Languages
practice_exercises.description: Compare Zip Slip impact across Java, Python, and JS.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Java: UNSAFE (no built-in path check)
Python: UNSAFE (extractall follows paths as-is)
Go: UNSAFE (archive/zip doesn't validate)
Node.js: UNSAFE (most libs don't check)
Fix: Validate canonical path of every entry before extraction.

hints.message: Most archive libraries in most languages do NOT validate paths.
The developer must add the check — it's not automatic.
NONE are safe by default. Every language needs manual canonical path validation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Design a Secure File Storage Service
practice_exercises.description: Architecture for a file storage service immune to traversal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Naming: UUID-based (never use original names)
Storage: /data/{user_id}/{uuid}.{ext}
Access: verify user_id matches session user (or admin)
Traversal: whitelist UUID format + canonical check + user isolation

hints.message: Never use original filenames for storage — generate UUIDs.
Isolate users in separate directories: /data/{user_id}/.
UUID names, user isolation, canonical checks, admin-only cross-user access.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Penetration Test Plan for Path Traversal
practice_exercises.description: Write a pen test checklist for path traversal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Test 1: ../../../etc/passwd -> 400/403
Test 2: %2e%2e%2f, %252e, %c0%af -> 400/403
Test 3: ..\..\windows\win.ini -> 400/403
Test 4: Upload ZIP with ../../../ entries -> extraction blocked
Test 5: Create symlink pointing outside dir -> access blocked

hints.message: Test all attack variants: direct, encoded, platform-specific.
Zip Slip needs a crafted ZIP file with malicious entry names.
5 test categories covering all known bypass techniques.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Fortinet VPN CVE-2018-13379
practice_exercises.description: Analyse the Fortinet path traversal that exposed VPN credentials.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Fortinet FortiOS SSL VPN
2. Path traversal in web portal
3. VPN session/credential files
4. Usernames and plaintext passwords for VPN users
5. Used to attack government agencies, hospitals, critical infrastructure
6. VPN credentials = direct network access, bypassing all other security

hints.message: The vulnerability exposed VPN login credentials.
VPN credentials give attackers direct access to internal networks.
Fortinet VPN traversal -> credentials stolen -> used against governments and hospitals. Patch management critical!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Build vs Buy: Path Validation Library
practice_exercises.description: Evaluate whether to build custom validation or use a library.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A: Pros=full control. Cons=easy to miss edge cases, maintenance burden.
B: Pros=well-tested, widely used. Cons=may not cover all cases.
C: Pros=security-focused, comprehensive. Cons=heavy dependency.
Recommend: Library (B or C) + canonical path check as backup.

hints.message: Custom code means custom bugs. Libraries have been tested by thousands.
Economy of Mechanism: simpler to use a tested library.
Use a library + canonical path check. Defence in depth!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Security Header Audit for File Server
practice_exercises.description: Check all security headers for a file-serving application.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. default-src 'none' (file downloads need no scripts)
2. nosniff (prevent MIME sniffing)
3. attachment; filename="safe_name" (force download)
4. DENY (prevent framing)
5. no-store, no-cache (prevent caching sensitive files)
6. application/octet-stream (force binary download)

hints.message: File downloads should force download (attachment) not render in browser.
nosniff prevents the browser from guessing content types.
All 6 headers work together to prevent XSS-through-download, MIME sniffing, and caching attacks.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Capstone: Complete Secure File Service
practice_exercises.description: Design and implement a complete secure file service.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Upload: UUID naming, type validation, size limit, scan for malware
Download: whitelist + canonical + user ownership check
Delete: admin only, audit log, soft delete first
List: user's own files only, no path parameter
All 8 design principles applied throughout.

hints.message: Every operation needs its own set of defences.
Upload is write, download is read, delete is destructive — each has different risks.
Complete service with all 8 principles, all defence layers, proper logging and headers.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Path Traversal CTF Challenge
practice_exercises.description: Solve a capture-the-flag style path traversal challenge.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attempt 1: ../../secret/flag.txt -> BLOCKED (contains ../)
Attempt 2: %2e%2e/%2e%2e/secret/flag.txt -> WORKS (filter checks literal ../ not encoded)
Attempt 3: ....//....//secret/flag.txt -> WORKS if filter removes ../ once

hints.message: The filter only checks for literal ../ characters.
URL encoding means the filter sees %2e%2e/ not ../.
Encoded and nested both bypass the literal check. From /opt/app/public/ need 2x ../ to reach /opt/.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Compliance: CWE-22 Remediation Report
practice_exercises.description: Write a formal remediation report for a discovered path traversal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios prepare you for real security work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
CWE-22, CVSS ~7.5 (High)
Description: User input in file path without validation
Impact: Arbitrary file read, potential credential exposure
Root cause: Missing input validation and canonical path check
Immediate: WAF rule to block ../ patterns
Long-term: Whitelist + canonical path + least privilege
Test: Pen test with all encoding variants
Timeline: WAF now, code fix within 48hrs, full audit within 2 weeks

hints.message: CVSS 7.5 is High severity for arbitrary file read.
Immediate mitigation = WAF rule. Long-term = code fix.
Complete report: ID, CWE, CVSS, description, impact, root cause, immediate fix, long-term fix, testing, timeline.
